author: @NorthernKitty
text: Looks VERY handy!!  And it looks VERY similar to the Ryobi BS904G sold here in the U.S., roughly same wattage.  Although the Ryobi doesn't have a fence, push stick or handle on top, and has a different base.  Great review, I've been wondering if little band saws like this are worth it!!   But why do I get the feeling a future video will be "how to make your own band saw out of <<insert unexpected junkyard item here>>"?   😊😊😊

author: @0.zz.y
text:
Hi Rob, I recently bought a budget bandsaw and at first I thought...ahhh damn, it cant cut straight, the blade wonders etc. I then watched a video on youtube from "The wood whisperer" called how to set up your bandsaw. Wow! What a difference. It doesn't take long and also gives you the knowledge how they should work and also how easy it is to "tune in". I recommend giving it a try mate.

Cheers,
Elliot

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - I will go have a look

author: @William_Hada
text: That's a very nice quality saw! Here's a useful tip. If you put a quality bimetal blade on it like this one : Starrett - 99124-06-08 Intenss Pro-Die Band Saw Blade, Bimetal, Intenss Tooth, Raker Set, Positive Rake, 80" Length, 3/8" Width, 0.025" Thick, 10-14 TPI ( Available on Amazon ), you can easily cut metals like aluminum, steel , stainless etc. I put one on mine and it cuts through these metals like butter. The key to this is using a quality blade like the Starrett. Lower quality ones will wear out fast and do a poor job. You will of course need to choose the proper length and width for your particular saw.

author: @htmagic
text: RMS, I'm using the bandsaw that my Dad built years ago. 2x4 frame, wooden pulleys, angle iron and skate wheel blade guide, table from their old orange countertop they replaced in their kitchen, belt drive and washing machine motor. And a series of light switches for on/off and crude speed control (hi. low). Although he passed away years ago, every time I use it, I think of him and am amazed how well it works.

author: @ches74
text: Probably for the best that the gear is nylon as it is easily replaced. It will take all the wear instead of sharing that wear with the more expensive to replace rack.

author: @reincarnatethylacine70
text: It might be a hobby band saw and there's plenty of help on setting setting them up on the net but my 2 cents tip is to buy professional quality blades, particularly if you want to cut aluminium. 2ndly it pays to have a range of tooth pitches and blade  depths handy as this can be the difference between success and frustration.

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text: _  “like that for instance” _ lever.  Think that is the quick-release blade tension lever. What would be helpful in a future episode is how to weld the blade back together again when it brakes (as it will do from time to time) and grind the join smooth. Also, show how to twist any spare blades into a smaller loop so they take up less space when hung on a wall or wherever. A visual demo of this technique is worth a thousand words. This is one of the machine tools that one doesn’t know how useful and time saving it is until you get one.  If the company gets the parts manufactured abroad it may be an idea to buy these capital items sooner than later, as foreign made equipment is due to go up in price (by a lot) as the current economic crises unfolds.

author: @creepycrawlything
text: Nice presentation of tool and tool-philosophy.

author: @MrAnderson4509
text: my students and I loved the band saw.  All most got me once, always remember ( tell Luke for me)  the is a small safety circle around the blade that is how far the blade will jump forward at you while putting a load on it from pushing the stock into the blade, also this circle includes the distance time it takes you to stop the motion of your hands from pushing to stop moving ( this is under estimated often, No matter how good your reflexes are, this last thing cannot be overcome, Leave more room than you think is needed, use the push stick when you can), I had two students injured and it was well working with a harder wood, pushing, and having the blade emerge from a cut sooner than they were ready With their hands too far in the circle of safety around the blade. You guys enjoy your new Bandsaw it looks like a pretty darn good one, keep your guides in adjustment Blade tension proper, and purchase some replacement guides if you have the type that are friction based on consumable, if you have ball bearing guides, find bearings with double lip seals to replace the ones they gave you, have Fun, this will be a great tool, they are very handy
